summary: Giovanni Andreozzi was my father's biological father who came from Falvaterra, Lazio, Italy to the United States in 1909. He settled in Lackawanna, New York, near the steel plant on the shores of Lake Erie. Giovanni married my grandmother Vincenza Limongiello in late 1916, and they had three children, including my father Filippo. Sadly, Giovanni died in November 1918 from the Spanish flu at Columbus Hospital in Buffalo, which was the Italian hospital at the time.

Falvaterra is the small town in Lazio, Italy, just south of Rome, where my paternal grandfather Giovanni Andreozzi was born. He left Falvaterra in 1909 to come to the United States, starting his new life in Buffalo and Lackawanna, New York. It was from this place that our family's American journey began.

Lazio is the region in Italy where my grandfather Giovanni Andreozzi was born, specifically in the town of Falvaterra. It's located just south of Rome. This region is part of my paternal family's roots before they emigrated to the United States in 1909.

The United States is where my family settled after emigrating from Italy. My paternal grandfather Giovanni arrived here in 1909, settling in Buffalo and Lackawanna, New York. My maternal grandfather Filiberto Covelli also moved back and forth between Italy and the US before my mother and her family finally came over in December 1927. The US was the land of opportunity for both sides of my family, despite the hardships they faced.

Vincenza Limongiello, who later Americanized her name to Jenny Limoncello, was my paternal grandmother. She was born in a tough waterfront tenement area called 'the Hooks' in Buffalo, New York. She married Giovanni Andreozzi in late 1916, and they had three children, including my father Filippo. Jenny endured the loss of her infant daughter Maria Giuseppe in 1918 and then the death of Giovanni later that same year.

The Spanish flu pandemic tragically affected my family in 1918. My grandfather Giovanni Andreozzi died suddenly in November 1918 from the flu while being treated at Columbus Hospital in Buffalo, which was the Italian hospital. This pandemic also claimed the life of my infant aunt Maria Giuseppe just a few months earlier in August 1918. The Spanish flu left a lasting mark on our family history.

Buffalo, New York was the city where my paternal grandmother Vincenza Limongiello was born, specifically in a rough waterfront neighborhood called 'the Hooks.' It was also near where my grandfather Giovanni Andreozzi arrived and settled after coming from Italy. Buffalo was home to Columbus Hospital, where Giovanni died during the Spanish flu pandemic in 1918. The city played a central role in my family's early American life.

Lackawanna, New York, was the town where my paternal family settled after arriving from Italy. It was located right on the shores of Lake Erie and was home to the largest steel plant in the world by 1900. My grandparents Giovanni and Vincenza lived there, and my father Filippo was born there. It was also where my family later placed markers on the graves of relatives who died during the Spanish flu in the Holy Cross Cemetery.

Lake Erie is the great lake on whose shores Lackawanna, New York is located, where my paternal family lived. The proximity to Lake Erie meant that Lackawanna was a hub for industry, including the massive steel plant built by 1900. This setting shaped much of my family's early life in America.

Columbus Hospital in Buffalo was the Italian hospital where my grandfather Giovanni Andreozzi was taken when he fell ill during the Spanish flu pandemic in 1918. He died there within a week of contracting the flu. This hospital was a significant place for the Italian immigrant community in Buffalo at that time.

Calabria is the region in southern Italy where my maternal grandmother Rosina Ruffalo was from. Specifically, she was born near Cosenza in a small town called Marano Principato. Calabria represents my mother's roots before she and her family emigrated to the United States in 1927.

Cosenza is the northern province of Calabria, Italy, near which my maternal grandmother Rosina Ruffalo was born. The small town of Marano Principato, her hometown, lies close to Cosenza. This area is central to my mother's family history before they moved to the United States.

Marano Principato is the small town in Calabria, Italy where my maternal grandmother Rosina Ruffalo was born. It is near Cosenza in the northern part of Calabria. My mother's family lived there until they emigrated to the United States in December 1927 after Rosina's death in 1925.

Filiberto Covelli was my maternal grandfather who moved back and forth between Italy and the United States starting in 1899. He initially went to Chicago, then worked in Kenosha, Wisconsin, before moving to Lackawanna, New York for better-paying jobs. Filiberto married Rosina Ruffalo, and after her sudden death in 1925, he remarried a woman named Gelsomina. He finally brought his family to the United States in December 1927.

Chicago was the first American city my maternal grandfather Filiberto Covelli went to after leaving Italy. Many Calabresi immigrants settled there, including his father who arrived in 1898. Filiberto later moved on to other places like Kenosha, Wisconsin, but Chicago was the starting point for his American journey.

Kenosha, Wisconsin was where my maternal grandfather Filiberto Covelli worked after moving from Chicago. There was a community of people from his hometown there, and he spent some time working in that area before relocating to Lackawanna, New York for better job opportunities. Kenosha was part of his back-and-forth migration between Italy and the United States.

Holy Cross Cemetery in Lackawanna, New York is where several members of my paternal family who died from the Spanish flu were buried. The graves were originally unmarked, so my father, siblings, and I pooled money to place markers there. This cemetery holds a lot of family history and memories for us.

Rosina Ruffalo was my maternal grandmother from Marano Principato, Calabria, Italy. She married Filiberto Covelli and had several children, including my mother. Unfortunately, Rosina died suddenly in March 1925, which deeply affected her children and family. Despite her early death, she remains a significant figure in our family history, often described as one of the 'ghosts' lingering in our memories.

Gelsomina was the woman my maternal grandfather Filiberto Covelli remarried after the death of my grandmother Rosina Ruffalo in 1925. After marrying Gelsomina, Filiberto finally brought his family, including my mother, to the United States in December 1927. Gelsomina became part of our family story during that transitional period.
